content:
# Acts Chapter 27 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 259-260 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/ACT_027_page_001.jpg, ACT_027_page_002.jpg

---

**1** And when it was decided that we should sail to Italy, they delivered Paul and certain other prisoners to a centurion named Julius, of the Augustan Cohort. **2** And embarking in a ship of Adramyttium, which was about to sail to the ports along the coast of Asia, we put to sea, Aristarchus, a Macedonian of Thessalonica, being with us. **3** And on the next day we put in at Sidon; and Julius, treating Paul kindly, permitted him to go to his friends and receive their care. **4** And putting to sea from there, we sailed under the lee of Cyprus, because the winds were contrary. **5** And having sailed across the open sea along Cilicia and Pamphylia, we came to Myra in Lycia.

**6** And there the centurion found a ship of Alexandria sailing to Italy, and he put us aboard it. **7** And sailing slowly for a good many days, and having arrived with difficulty off Cnidus, the wind not permitting us to go farther, we sailed under the lee of Crete off Salmone. **8** And coasting along it with difficulty, we came to a certain place called Fair Havens, near which was the city of Lasea.

**9** And when considerable time had passed and the voyage was now dangerous, because even the Fast had already gone by, Paul advised them, **10** saying, "Men, I perceive that the voyage will be with injury and much loss, not only of the cargo and the ship, but also of our lives." **11** But the centurion was persuaded by the pilot and the shipowner rather than by what was spoken by Paul. **12** And because the harbor was unsuitable for wintering, the majority reached a decision to put to sea from there, if somehow they could reach Phoenix, a harbor of Crete facing southwest and northwest, and winter there.

**13** And when a south wind blew gently, supposing that they had obtained their purpose, they weighed anchor and sailed along Crete, close to the shore. **14** But not long after, there rushed down from it a tempestuous wind called the Northeaster. **15** And when the ship was caught and could not face the wind, we gave way and were driven along. **16** And running under the lee of a small island called Cauda, we were scarcely able to gain control of the ship's boat. **17** After hoisting it up, they used supports to undergird the ship; and fearing that they would run aground on the Syrtis, they lowered the gear and so were driven along. **18** And as we were being violently storm-tossed, on the next day they began to jettison the cargo. **19** And on the third day, with their own hands they threw the tackle of the ship overboard. **20** And when neither sun nor stars appeared for many days, and no small storm was pressing upon us, from then on all hope that we would be saved was taken away.

**21** And when they had gone a long time without food, then Paul stood in their midst and said, "Men, you ought to have listened to me and not have set sail from Crete, and so avoided this injury and loss. **22** And now I urge you to take heart, for there will be no loss of life among you, but only of the ship. **23** For there stood by me this night an angel of the God to whom I belong and whom I serve, **24** saying, 'Do not be afraid, Paul; you must stand before Caesar. And behold, God has granted you all those who sail with you.' **25** Therefore take heart, men, for I believe God that it will be exactly as it has been told to me. **26** But we must run aground on a certain island."

**27** And when the fourteenth night had come, as we were being driven about in the Adriatic Sea, about midnight the sailors suspected that some land was approaching them. **28** And taking soundings, they found twenty fathoms; and having gone a little farther, they took soundings again and found fifteen fathoms. **29** And fearing that we might run aground on rocky places, they cast four anchors from the stern and prayed for day to come. **30** And when the sailors were seeking to flee from the ship and had lowered the boat into the sea under pretense that they were going to lay out anchors from the bow, **31** Paul said to the centurion and to the soldiers, "Unless these men remain in the ship, you yourselves cannot be saved." **32** Then the soldiers cut the ropes of the boat and let it fall away.

**33** And until the day was about to dawn, Paul kept urging them all to take food, saying, "Today is the fourteenth day that you have continued watching and going without food, having taken nothing. **34** Therefore I urge you to take some food, for this is for your preservation; for not a hair from the head of any of you shall perish." **35** And having said these things, he took bread and gave thanks to God in the presence of all, and he broke it and began to eat. **36** And they all became encouraged and themselves also took food. **37** And we were in all two hundred and seventy-six souls in the ship. **38** And when they had eaten enough, they began to lighten the ship by throwing the wheat into the sea.

**39** And when day came, they did not recognize the land; but they observed a certain bay having a beach, onto which they planned, if possible, to drive the ship ashore. **40** And casting off the anchors, they left them in the sea, at the same time loosening the bands of the rudders; and hoisting the foresail to the wind, they made for the beach. **41** But striking a place where two seas met, they ran the vessel aground; and the prow stuck fast and remained immovable, but the stern was being broken apart by the force of the waves.

**42** Now the plan of the soldiers was to kill the prisoners, lest any of them should swim away and escape. **43** But the centurion, wanting to save Paul, kept them from their purpose, and commanded those who were able to swim to throw themselves overboard first and get to land, **44** and the rest, some on planks and some on pieces from the ship. And so it came about that all were brought safely to land.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

1. **v.1 — "Augustan Cohort" (σπειρης Σεβαστης):** Rendered as "Augustan Cohort," transliterating the honorific title. Σεβαστης (Sebastes) is the Greek equivalent of the Latin "Augustus." Sinaiticus preserves the standard reading here.

2. **v.9 — "the Fast" (την νηστειαν):** This refers to the Day of Atonement (Yom Kippur), which falls in September-October. Retained as "the Fast" to preserve the formal reference as it appears in the Greek, rather than inserting an explanatory gloss.

3. **v.14 — "Northeaster" (Ευρακυλων):** Sinaiticus reads ΕΥΡΑΚΥΛΩΝ, a compound of Euros (east wind) and Aquilo (Latin, north wind). This hybrid Greco-Latin term designates a violent northeast gale. Rendered "Northeaster" as the closest formal equivalent.

4. **v.16 — "Cauda" (Καυδα):** Sinaiticus reads ΚΑΥΔΑ rather than the variant ΚΛΑΥΔΑ (Clauda) found in some manuscripts. The LOGOS Bible follows the Sinaiticus reading.

5. **v.17 — "Syrtis" (Συρτιν):** The sandbanks off the North African coast, greatly feared by ancient sailors. Retained in transliterated form as a proper noun.

6. **v.27 — "Adriatic Sea" (Αδρια):** In ancient usage, the Adriatic (Adria) encompassed the central Mediterranean between Italy, Greece, and North Africa — broader than the modern Adriatic Sea. Rendered literally without geographic correction.

7. **v.37 — "two hundred and seventy-six" (διακοσιαι εβδομηκοντα εξ):** Sinaiticus reads 276 (ΔΙΑΚΟΣΙΑΙ ΕΒΔΟΜΗΚΟΝΤΑ ΕΞ). Some manuscripts read 76, but the Sinaiticus reading of 276 is followed here.

8. **v.40 — "bands of the rudders" (ζευκτηριας των πηδαλιων):** Ancient ships had two large steering oars (rudders) that were lashed in place during anchoring. The crew loosened these bands to regain steering control. Rendered formally as "bands of the rudders."

9. **v.41 — "a place where two seas met" (τοπον διθαλασσον):** The Greek διθαλασσον literally means "between two seas" — likely a sandbar or reef where two currents converge. Rendered with the literal sense preserved.

10. **General — "we" passages:** Acts 27 is entirely narrated in the first-person plural, indicating the author's (Luke's) presence during the voyage. This "we" is preserved throughout without alteration.

— LOGOS
